question_text: Rain was falling vertically with a speed of 35 m/s. A wind started blowing after some time with a speed of 12 m/s in East to West direction. In which direction should a boy waiting at a bus stop hold his umbrella?
question_html: Rain was falling vertically with a speed of 35 m/s. A wind started blowing after some time with a speed of 12 m/s in East to West direction. In which direction should a boy waiting at a bus stop hold his umbrella?
solution_text: As shown in the figure the velocity of rain, V R = OA = 35 m/s, and the velocity of wind, V w = OB =12 m/s.The resultant velocity of raindrops will be the vector sum of the raindrop speed (vertical) and the horizontal wind speed. Here, OC represents the direction of resultant in which the boy should hold his umbrella, Hence, the magnitude of the resultant velocity is {tex}v = \sqrt { \left( v _ { R } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( v _ { W } \right) ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { ( 35 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 12 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = 37 m/s Let the resultant velocity, v (=OC) make an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the vertical. Then, {tex}\tan \theta = \frac { A C } { O A } = \frac { v _ { W } } { v _ { R } } = \frac { 12 } { 35 } = 0.343{/tex} {tex}\therefore \theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } ( 0.343 ) \simeq 19 ^ { \circ }{/tex} with respect to the direction of rainfall.
solution_html:
As shown in the figure the velocity of rain, VR = OA = 35 m/s, and the velocity of wind, Vw = OB =12 m/s.The resultant velocity of raindrops will be the vector sum of the raindrop speed (vertical) and the horizontal wind speed.  Here, OC represents the direction of resultant in which the boy should hold his umbrella,

Hence, the magnitude of the resultant velocity is
{tex}v = \sqrt { \left( v _ { R } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( v _ { W } \right) ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { ( 35 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 12 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}= 37 m/s
Let the resultant velocity, v (=OC) make an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the vertical. Then,
{tex}\tan \theta = \frac { A C } { O A } = \frac { v _ { W } } { v _ { R } } = \frac { 12 } { 35 } = 0.343{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } ( 0.343 ) \simeq 19 ^ { \circ }{/tex} with respect to the direction of rainfall.

question_text: Find the magnitude and direction of the resultant of two vectors A and B in terms of their magnitudes and angle {tex}\theta{/tex} between them.
question_html: Find the magnitude and direction of the resultant of two vectors A and B in terms of their magnitudes and angle {tex}\theta{/tex} between them.
solution_text: Let the vectors OP and OQ represent two vectors A and B which are at angle θ with each other as shown in figure, As we know the magnitude of the resultant vector R is given by parallelogram law of vector addition as {tex}R = \sqrt { A ^ { 2 } + B ^ { 2 } + 2 A B \cos \theta }{/tex} This is known as law of cosine. Now, from the figure, resultant vector R makes an angle {tex}\alpha{/tex} with vector A. Then, for right angled triangles we have, In {tex}\Delta O S N{/tex} , SN = OS sin {tex}\alpha{/tex} = R sin {tex}\alpha{/tex} and In {tex}\Delta P S N{/tex} , SN = PS sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = B sin {tex}\theta{/tex} and {tex}\Rightarrow\frac { R } { \sin \theta } = \frac { B } { \sin \alpha }{/tex} ..............(i) Similarly, PM = Asin {tex}\alpha{/tex} = B sin {tex}\beta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { A } { \sin \beta } = \frac { B } { \sin \alpha }{/tex} ........................(ii) From Eqs.(i) and (ii), we get {tex}\frac { R } { \sin \theta } = \frac { A } { \sin \beta } = \frac { B } { \sin \alpha } {/tex} This is known as law of sines. Law of cosine gives the value of magnitude of two vectors and law of sines provide the information about the angle / directions.
solution_html:
Let the vectors OP and OQ represent two vectors A and B which are at angle θ with each other as shown in figure,

As we know the magnitude of the resultant vector R is given by parallelogram law of vector addition as 
{tex}R = \sqrt { A ^ { 2 } + B ^ { 2 } + 2 A B \cos \theta }{/tex}
This is known as law of cosine.
Now, from the figure, resultant vector R makes an angle {tex}\alpha{/tex} with vector A. Then, for right angled triangles we have, 
In {tex}\Delta O S N{/tex}, SN = OS sin{tex}\alpha{/tex} = R sin{tex}\alpha{/tex} and
In {tex}\Delta P S N{/tex}, SN = PS sin{tex}\theta{/tex} = B sin{tex}\theta{/tex} and
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac { R } { \sin \theta } = \frac { B } { \sin \alpha }{/tex}..............(i)
Similarly,
PM = Asin{tex}\alpha{/tex} = B sin{tex}\beta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { A } { \sin \beta } = \frac { B } { \sin \alpha }{/tex}........................(ii)
From Eqs.(i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\frac { R } { \sin \theta } = \frac { A } { \sin \beta } = \frac { B } { \sin \alpha } {/tex}
This is known as law of sines.
Law of cosine gives the value of magnitude of two vectors and law of sines provide the information about the angle / directions.

question_text: A motor boat is racing towards North at 25 km /h and the water current in that region is 10 km /h in the direction of 60° East to South. Find the resultant velocity of the boat.
question_html: A motor boat is racing towards North at 25 km /h and the water current in that region is 10 km /h in the direction of 60° East to South. Find the resultant velocity of the boat.
solution_text: Given, velocity of motor boat, v b = 25 km/h (towards North) Velocity of water current, v c = 10 km/h (towards East to South) angle between the motor boat and water current is, {tex}\theta = 180 ^ { \circ } - 60 ^ { \circ } = 120 ^ { \circ }{/tex} We know that, from parallelogram law of vector addition, the resultant vector v {tex}v = \sqrt { v _ { b } ^ { 2 } + v _ { c } ^ { 2 } + 2 v _ { b } v _ { c } \cos \theta }{/tex} {tex}v = \sqrt { ( 25 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 10 ) ^ { 2 } + 2 \times 25 \times 10 \times \cos 120 ^ { \circ } }{/tex} {tex}v = \sqrt { 625 + 100 + 500 \left( - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) }{/tex} velocity of the water boat with respect to the water current = 21.8 km/h Suppose the resultant velocity v makes angle {tex}\phi{/tex} with the North direction. Then, {tex}\tan \phi = \frac { v _ { c } \sin 120 ^ { \circ } } { v _ { b } + v _ { c } \cos 120 ^ { \circ } } = \frac { 10 \times ( \sqrt { 3 } / 2 ) } { 25 + 10 \times ( - 1 / 2 ) } = 0.433{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \phi = \tan ^ { - 1 } ( 0.433 ) = 23.4 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, velocity of motor boat, vb = 25 km/h (towards North)
Velocity of water current, vc = 10 km/h (towards East to South)
angle between the motor boat and water current is, {tex}\theta = 180 ^ { \circ } - 60 ^ { \circ } = 120 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
We know that, from parallelogram law of vector addition, the resultant vector v
{tex}v = \sqrt { v _ { b } ^ { 2 } + v _ { c } ^ { 2 } + 2 v _ { b } v _ { c } \cos \theta }{/tex}
{tex}v = \sqrt { ( 25 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 10 ) ^ { 2 } + 2 \times 25 \times 10 \times \cos 120 ^ { \circ } }{/tex}
{tex}v = \sqrt { 625 + 100 + 500 \left( - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) }{/tex}
velocity of the water boat with respect to the water current = 21.8 km/h
Suppose the resultant velocity v makes angle {tex}\phi{/tex} with the North direction. Then,
{tex}\tan \phi = \frac { v _ { c } \sin 120 ^ { \circ } } { v _ { b } + v _ { c } \cos 120 ^ { \circ } } = \frac { 10 \times ( \sqrt { 3 } / 2 ) } { 25 + 10 \times ( - 1 / 2 ) } = 0.433{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \phi = \tan ^ { - 1 } ( 0.433 ) = 23.4 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

question_text: The position of a particle is given by r = {tex}3.0 t \hat { \mathrm { i } } + 2.0 t ^ { 2 } \hat { \mathrm { j } } + 5.0 \hat { \mathrm { k } }{/tex} where t is in seconds and the coefficients have the proper units for r to be in metres. Find v(t) and a(t) of the particles. Find the magnitude and the direction of v(t) at t = 1.0 s.
question_html:
The position of a particle is given by r = {tex}3.0 t \hat { \mathrm { i } } + 2.0 t ^ { 2 } \hat { \mathrm { j } } + 5.0 \hat { \mathrm { k } }{/tex} where t is in seconds and the coefficients have the proper units for r to be in metres.

  1. Find v(t) and a(t) of the particles.
  2. Find the magnitude and the direction of v(t) at t = 1.0 s.
solution_text: velocity is given by {tex}\mathbf{v}(t)=\frac{d \mathbf{r}}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}\left(3.0 t \hat{\mathbf{i}}+2.0 t^{2} \hat{\mathbf{j}}+5.0 \hat{\mathbf{k}}\right){/tex} =3.0 {tex}\hat{\mathbf{i}}{/tex} +4.0 t {tex}\hat{\mathbf{j}}{/tex} and acceleration is given by {tex}\mathbf{a}(t)=\frac{d \mathbf{v}}{d t}=+4.0 \hat{\mathbf{j}}{/tex} a = 4.0 m s –2 along y- direction At t = 1.0 s,=3.0 {tex}\hat{\mathbf{i}}{/tex} +4.0 t {tex}\hat{\mathbf{j}}{/tex} It’s magnitude is {tex}v=\sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}}{/tex} = 5.0 ms -1 and direction is {tex}\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{v_{y}}{v_{x}}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \cong 53^{\circ}{/tex} with x-axis.
solution_html:
1. velocity is given by  {tex}\mathbf{v}(t)=\frac{d \mathbf{r}}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}\left(3.0 t \hat{\mathbf{i}}+2.0 t^{2} \hat{\mathbf{j}}+5.0 \hat{\mathbf{k}}\right){/tex}
           =3.0 {tex}\hat{\mathbf{i}}{/tex}+4.0 t {tex}\hat{\mathbf{j}}{/tex} and acceleration is given by 
    {tex}\mathbf{a}(t)=\frac{d \mathbf{v}}{d t}=+4.0 \hat{\mathbf{j}}{/tex}
         a = 4.0 m s–2 along y- direction
         At t = 1.0 s,=3.0 {tex}\hat{\mathbf{i}}{/tex}+4.0 t {tex}\hat{\mathbf{j}}{/tex}
  2. It’s magnitude is {tex}v=\sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}}{/tex} = 5.0 ms-1
         and direction is {tex}\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{v_{y}}{v_{x}}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \cong 53^{\circ}{/tex} with x-axis.

question_text: A particle starts from origin at t = 0 with a velocity 5 {tex}\hat i{/tex} m/s and moves in xy-plane under action of a force which produces a constant acceleration of (3 {tex}\hat i {/tex} + 2 {tex}\hat j {/tex} ) m/s 2 . What is the y-coordinate of the particle at the instant its x-coordinate is 84 m? What is the speed of the particle at this time?
question_html:
A particle starts from origin at t = 0 with a velocity 5{tex}\hat i{/tex} m/s and moves in xy-plane under action of a force which produces a constant acceleration of (3{tex}\hat i {/tex} + 2{tex}\hat j {/tex}) m/s2.

  1. What is the y-coordinate of the particle at the instant its x-coordinate is 84 m?
  2. What is the speed of the particle at this time?
solution_text: Given, Initial velocity u = {tex}5 \hat { \mathbf { i } } \mathrm { m } / \mathrm { s } , a = (3 \hat { \mathbf { i } } + 2 \hat { \mathbf { j } }) \mathrm { m } / \mathrm { s } ^ { 2 }{/tex} Then from equation of kinematics, r(t) = {tex}v _ { 0 } t + \frac { 1 } { 2 } a t ^ { 2 }{/tex} and {tex}v = \sqrt { v _ { x } ^ { 2 } + v _ { y } ^ { 2 } }{/tex} r(t) = {tex}= 5 \hat { \mathbf { i } } t + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( 3 \hat { \mathbf { i } } + 2 \hat { \mathbf { j } } ) t ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \left( 5 t + \frac { 3 } { 2 } t ^ { 2 } \right) \hat { \mathbf { i } } + t ^ { 2 } \hat { \mathbf { j } }{/tex} On comparing the corresponding terms, x(t) = {tex}5 t + \frac { 3 } { 2 } t ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(t) = 1t 2 The speed of the particle can be calculated by differentiating the position vector w.r.t time. If x(t) = 84 m, then t = 6 s {tex}\therefore{/tex} y(6) = 36 m {tex}v = \frac { d y } { d t } = \frac { d \left( 5 t + \frac { 3 } { 2 } t ^ { 2 } \right) \hat { \mathbf { i } } + t ^ { 2 } \hat { \mathbf { j } } } { d t }{/tex} {tex}= ( 5 + 3 t ) \hat { \mathbf { i } } + 2 \hat { t } \hat { \mathbf { j } }{/tex} At t = 6 s, v = {tex}23 \hat { \mathbf { i } } + 12 \hat { \mathbf { j } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore v = \sqrt { ( 23 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 12 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = 26 m/s
solution_html:
Given,
Initial velocity u = {tex}5 \hat { \mathbf { i } } \mathrm { m } / \mathrm { s } , a = (3 \hat { \mathbf { i } } + 2 \hat { \mathbf { j } }) \mathrm { m } / \mathrm { s } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
Then from equation of kinematics,

r(t) = {tex}v _ { 0 } t + \frac { 1 } { 2 } a t ^ { 2 }{/tex} and {tex}v = \sqrt { v _ { x } ^ { 2 } + v _ { y } ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
r(t) = {tex}= 5 \hat { \mathbf { i } } t + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( 3 \hat { \mathbf { i } } + 2 \hat { \mathbf { j } } ) t ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \left( 5 t + \frac { 3 } { 2 } t ^ { 2 } \right) \hat { \mathbf { i } } + t ^ { 2 } \hat { \mathbf { j } }{/tex}
On comparing the corresponding terms,

x(t) = {tex}5 t + \frac { 3 } { 2 } t ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(t) = 1t2

  1.  The speed of the particle can be calculated by differentiating the position vector w.r.t time.
    If x(t) = 84 m, then t = 6 s
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} y(6) = 36 m
  2. {tex}v = \frac { d y } { d t } = \frac { d \left( 5 t + \frac { 3 } { 2 } t ^ { 2 } \right) \hat { \mathbf { i } } + t ^ { 2 } \hat { \mathbf { j } } } { d t }{/tex}
    {tex}= ( 5 + 3 t ) \hat { \mathbf { i } } + 2 \hat { t } \hat { \mathbf { j } }{/tex}
    At t = 6 s, v = {tex}23 \hat { \mathbf { i } } + 12 \hat { \mathbf { j } }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore v = \sqrt { ( 23 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 12 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}= 26 m/s

question_text: Galileo, in his book Two new sciences , stated that for elevations that exceed or fall short of 45 by equal amounts, the ranges are equal. Prove this statement.
question_html: Galileo, in his book Two new sciences, stated that for elevations that exceed or fall short of 45 by equal amounts, the ranges are equal. Prove this statement.
solution_text: For a projectile launched with velocity v o at an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} o , the range is given by {tex}R=\frac{v_{0}^{2} \sin 2 \theta_{0}}{g}{/tex} . Now, for angles, (45° + {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) and ( 45° – {tex}\alpha{/tex} ), 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} o is (90° + 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) and ( 90° – 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} ), respectively. The values of sin (90° + 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) and sin (90° – 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) are the same, equal to that of cos 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} . Therefore, ranges are equal for elevations that exceed or fall short of 45° by equal amounts {tex}\alpha{/tex} .
solution_html:
For a projectile launched with velocity vo at an angle {tex}\theta{/tex}o, the range is given by {tex}R=\frac{v_{0}^{2} \sin 2 \theta_{0}}{g}{/tex}.
Now, for angles, (45° + {tex}\alpha{/tex}) and ( 45° – {tex}\alpha{/tex}), 2{tex}\theta{/tex}o is (90° + 2{tex}\alpha{/tex}) and ( 90° – 2{tex}\alpha{/tex}), respectively. The values of sin (90° + 2{tex}\alpha{/tex}) and sin (90° – 2{tex}\alpha{/tex}) are the same, equal to that of cos 2{tex}\alpha{/tex}. Therefore, ranges are equal for elevations that exceed or fall short of 45° by equal amounts {tex}\alpha{/tex}.

question_text: A hiker stands on the edge of a cliff 490 m above the ground and throws a stone horizontally with an initial speed of 15 m s -1 . Neglecting air resistance, find the time taken by the stone to reach the ground and the speed with which it hits the ground. (Take g = 9.8 m s 2 .
question_html: A hiker stands on the edge of a cliff 490 m above the ground and throws a stone horizontally with an initial speed of 15 m s-1. Neglecting air resistance, find the time taken by the stone to reach the ground and the speed with which it hits the ground. (Take g = 9.8 m s2.
solution_text: Given, height, h = 490 m, initial velocity u x =15 m/s, a y = g = 9.8 m/s, a x = 0, u y = 0 Time taken by the stone under the freefall is given by, {tex}t = \sqrt { \frac { 2 h } { g } } = \sqrt { \frac { 2 \times 490 } { 9.8 } } = 10 \mathrm { s }{/tex} v x = u x + a x t = 15 + 0(10) = 15 m/s v y = u y + a y t = 0 + 9.8(10) = 98 m/s {tex}v = \sqrt { v _ { x } ^ { 2 } + v _ { y } ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { 15 ^ { 2 } + 98 ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = 99.14 m/s, with which it will hit the ground
solution_html:
Given, height, h = 490 m, initial velocity ux =15 m/s,
ay = g = 9.8 m/s, ax = 0, uy = 0

Time taken by the stone under the freefall is given by, 
{tex}t = \sqrt { \frac { 2 h } { g } } = \sqrt { \frac { 2 \times 490 } { 9.8 } } = 10 \mathrm { s }{/tex}
vx = ux + axt = 15 + 0(10) = 15 m/s
vy = uy + ayt = 0 + 9.8(10) = 98 m/s
{tex}v = \sqrt { v _ { x } ^ { 2 } + v _ { y } ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { 15 ^ { 2 } + 98 ^ { 2 } }{/tex}  = 99.14 m/s, with which it will hit the ground

question_text: A cricket ball is thrown at a speed of 28 m s -1 in a direction 30 above the horizontal. Calculate the maximum height the time taken by the ball to return to the same level and the distance from the thrower to the point where the ball returns to the same level.
question_html:
A cricket ball is thrown at a speed of 28 m s-1 in a direction 30 above the horizontal. Calculate

  1. the maximum height
  2. the time taken by the ball to return to the same level and
  3. the distance from the thrower to the point where the ball returns to the same level.
solution_text: The maximum height attained by the ball is given by the equation, {tex}H _ { m } = \frac { \left( v _ { 0 } \sin \theta _ { 0 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 g }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \left( 28 \sin 30 ^ { \circ } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 ( 9.8 ) } = \frac { 14 \times 14 } { 2 \times 9.8 }{/tex} = 10.0 m The time taken by the ball to return to the same level, i.e. time of flight is given by the equation T = {tex}\left( 2 v _ { 0 } \sin \theta _ { 0 } \right) / g = \left( 2 \times 28 \times \sin 30 ^ { \circ } \right) / 9.8{/tex} = 28/9.8 = 2.9 s The distance from the thrower to the point where the ball returns to the same level is the range of the projectile given by the relation R = {tex}\frac { \left( v _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \sin 2 \theta _ { 0 } \right) } { g } = \frac { 28 \times 28 \times \sin 60 ^ { \circ } } { 9.8 }{/tex} = 69.3 m
solution_html:
1. The maximum height attained by the ball is given by the equation,
    {tex}H _ { m } = \frac { \left( v _ { 0 } \sin \theta _ { 0 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 g }{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { \left( 28 \sin 30 ^ { \circ } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 ( 9.8 ) } = \frac { 14 \times 14 } { 2 \times 9.8 }{/tex}= 10.0 m
  2. The time taken by the ball to return to the same level, i.e. time of flight is given by the equation
    T ={tex}\left( 2 v _ { 0 } \sin \theta _ { 0 } \right) / g = \left( 2 \times 28 \times \sin 30 ^ { \circ } \right) / 9.8{/tex}
    = 28/9.8 = 2.9 s
  3. The distance from the thrower to the point where the ball returns to the same level is the range of the projectile given by the relation
    R = {tex}\frac { \left( v _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \sin 2 \theta _ { 0 } \right) } { g } = \frac { 28 \times 28 \times \sin 60 ^ { \circ } } { 9.8 }{/tex}= 69.3 m

subject: Physics
question_text: An insect trapped in a circular groove of radius 12 cm moves along the groove steadily and completes 7 revolutions in 100 s. What is the angular speed and the linear speed of insect's motion? Is the acceleration vector a constant vector? What is its magnitude?
question_html:
An insect trapped in a circular groove of radius 12 cm moves along the groove steadily and completes 7 revolutions in 100 s.

  1. What is the angular speed and the linear speed of insect's motion?
  2. Is the acceleration vector a constant vector? What is its magnitude?
solution_text: The given question is based on uniform circular motion. Here, radius of the circular track, R = 12 cm. The angular speed is given by the equation {tex}\omega = 2 \pi / T = 2 \pi \times 7 / 100 = 0.44 \mathrm { rad } / \mathrm { s }{/tex} and linear speed v is given as v = {tex}\omega R = 0.44 \times 12 = 5.3 \mathrm { cm } \mathrm { s } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} The direction of velocity v is along the tangent to the circle at every point. The acceleration is directed towards the centre of the circle (centripetal acceleration) and as the position of the particle changes continuously hence it is not a constant vector. However, the magnitude of acceleration is constant which is given by; a = {tex}\omega ^ { 2 } R = ( 0.44 ) ^ { 2 } \times 12 = 2.3 \mathrm { cm } \mathrm { s } ^ { - 2 }{/tex}
solution_html:
The given question is based on uniform circular motion.
Here, radius of the circular track, R = 12 cm.
The angular speed is given by the equation
{tex}\omega = 2 \pi / T = 2 \pi \times 7 / 100 = 0.44 \mathrm { rad } / \mathrm { s }{/tex}
and linear speed v is given as

v = {tex}\omega R = 0.44 \times 12 = 5.3 \mathrm { cm } \mathrm { s } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}
The direction of velocity v is along the tangent to the circle at every point.

The acceleration is directed towards the centre of the circle (centripetal acceleration) and as the position of the particle changes continuously hence it is not a constant vector.
However, the magnitude of acceleration is constant which is given by;
a = {tex}\omega ^ { 2 } R = ( 0.44 ) ^ { 2 } \times 12 = 2.3 \mathrm { cm } \mathrm { s } ^ { - 2 }{/tex}

question_text: State, for each of the following physical quantities, if it is a scalar or a vector: volume, mass, speed, acceleration, density, number of moles, velocity, angular frequency, displacement, angular velocity.
question_html: State, for each of the following physical quantities, if it is a scalar or a vector: volume, mass, speed, acceleration, density, number of moles, velocity, angular frequency, displacement, angular velocity.
solution_text: A scalar quantity is specified by its magnitude only. It does not have any direction associated with it. Hence, volume, mass, speed, density, number of moles, and angular frequency are scalar physical quantities. A vector quantity is specified by its magnitude as well as the direction associated with it. Hence, acceleration, velocity, displacement, and angular velocity belong to this category.
solution_html:
A scalar quantity is specified by its magnitude only. It does not have any direction associated with it. Hence, volume, mass, speed, density, number of moles, and angular frequency are scalar physical quantities.
A vector quantity is specified by its magnitude as well as the direction associated with it. Hence, acceleration, velocity, displacement, and angular velocity belong to this category.

subject: Physics
question_text: Pick out the two scalar quantities in the following list: force, angular momentum, work, current, linear momentum, electric field, average velocity, magnetic moment, relative velocity.
question_html:
Pick out the two scalar quantities in the following list:
force, angular momentum, work, current, linear momentum, electric field, average velocity, magnetic moment, relative velocity.
solution_text: Work and current are scalar quantities. Work done is given by the dot product of force and displacement. Since the dot product of two quantities is always a scalar, work is a scalar physical quantity. Current is described only by its magnitude. Its direction is not taken into account. Hence, it is a scalar quantity.
solution_html:
Work and current are scalar quantities.
Work done is given by the dot product of force and displacement. Since the dot product of two quantities is always a scalar, work is a scalar physical quantity.
Current is described only by its magnitude. Its direction is not taken into account. Hence, it is a scalar quantity.

question_text: Pick out the only vector quantity in the following list: Temperature, pressure, impulse, time, power, total path length, energy, gravitational potential, coefficient of friction, charge.
question_html: Pick out the only vector quantity in the following list: Temperature, pressure, impulse, time, power, total path length, energy, gravitational potential, coefficient of friction, charge.
solution_text: Impulse: Impulse is given by the product of force and time. Since force is a vector quantity, its product with time (a scalar quantity) gives a vector quantity.
solution_html: Impulse: Impulse is given by the product of force and time. Since force is a vector quantity, its product with time (a scalar quantity) gives a vector quantity.

question_text: State with reasons, whether the following algebraic operations with scalar and vector physical quantities are meaningful: adding any two scalars, adding a scalar to a vector of the same dimensions, multiplying any vector by any scalar, multiplying any two scalars, adding any two vectors, adding a component of a vector to the same vector.
question_html:
State with reasons, whether the following algebraic operations with scalar and vector physical quantities are meaningful:

  1. adding any two scalars,
  2. adding a scalar to a vector of the same dimensions,
  3. multiplying any vector by any scalar,
  4. multiplying any two scalars,
  5. adding any two vectors,
  6. adding a component of a vector to the same vector.
solution_text: The addition of two scalar quantities is meaningful only if they both represent the same physical quantity. The addition of a vector quantity with a scalar quantity is not meaningful. A scalar can be multiplied with a vector. For example, force is multiplied with time to give impulse. A scalar, irrespective of the physical quantity, can be multiplied with another scalar having the same or different dimensions. The addition of two vector quantities is meaningful only if they both represent the same physical quantity. A component of a vector can be added to the same vector as they both have the same dimensions.
solution_html:
1. The addition of two scalar quantities is meaningful only if they both represent the same physical quantity.
  2. The addition of a vector quantity with a scalar quantity is not meaningful.
  3. A scalar can be multiplied with a vector. For example, force is multiplied with time to give impulse.
  4. A scalar, irrespective of the physical quantity, can be multiplied with another scalar having the same or different dimensions.
  5. The addition of two vector quantities is meaningful only if they both represent the same physical quantity.
  6. A component of a vector can be added to the same vector as they both have the same dimensions.

question_text: Establish the |a + b| {tex}\leq{/tex} |a| + |b| vector inequalities geometrically or otherwise. When does this equality sign apply?
question_html: Establish the |a + b| {tex}\leq{/tex} |a| + |b| vector inequalities geometrically or otherwise. When does this equality sign apply?
solution_text: Let two vectors {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} be represented by the adjacent sides of a parallelogram OMNP, as shown in the given figure. Here, we can write: {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{OM}}|=|\mathrm{\vec a}|{/tex} ....(i) {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{MN}}|=|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}}|=|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|{/tex} ....(ii) {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{ON}}|=|{\vec a}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}|{/tex} ....(iii) In a triangle, each side is smaller than the sum of the other two sides. Therefore, in ΔOMN, we have: OM + MN > ON {tex}|\vec {ON}|{/tex} < {tex}|\vec {OM}| + |\vec {MN}|{/tex} {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}| <|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|+|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|{/tex} ....(iv) If the two vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} act along a straight line in the same direction, then we can write: {tex}|\vec{a}+\vec{b}|=|\vec{a}|+|\vec{b}|{/tex} ....(v) Combining equations (iv) and (v), we get: {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}|\leq| \vec{\mathrm{a}}|+| \vec{\mathrm{b}}|{/tex} This equality applies, if a and b are acting in the same direction e.g. angle between them = 0.
solution_html:
Let two vectors {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} be represented by the adjacent sides of a parallelogram OMNP, as shown in the given figure.

Here, we can write:
{tex}|\vec{\mathrm{OM}}|=|\mathrm{\vec a}|{/tex} ....(i)
{tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{MN}}|=|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}}|=|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|{/tex} ....(ii)
{tex}|\vec{\mathrm{ON}}|=|{\vec a}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}|{/tex} ....(iii)
In a triangle, each side is smaller than the sum of the other two sides.
Therefore, in ΔOMN, we have:
OM + MN > ON
{tex}|\vec {ON}|{/tex} < {tex}|\vec {OM}| + |\vec {MN}|{/tex}
{tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}| <|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|+|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|{/tex} ....(iv)
If the two vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} act along a straight line in the same direction, then we can write:
{tex}|\vec{a}+\vec{b}|=|\vec{a}|+|\vec{b}|{/tex} ....(v)
Combining equations (iv) and (v), we get:
{tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}|\leq| \vec{\mathrm{a}}|+| \vec{\mathrm{b}}|{/tex}
This equality applies, if a and b are acting in the same direction e.g. angle between them = 0.

question_text: Given {tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex} = 0, which of the following statements are correct: {tex}\vec a{/tex} , {tex}\vec b{/tex} , {tex}\vec c{/tex} , and {tex}\vec d{/tex} must each be a null vector. The magnitude of ( {tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} ) equals the magnitude of ( {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex} ). The magnitude of ' {tex}\vec a{/tex} ' can never be greater than the sum of the magnitudes of {tex}\vec b{/tex} , {tex}\vec c{/tex} , and {tex}\vec d{/tex} . {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} must lie in the plane of {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec d{/tex} if {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec d{/tex} are not collinear, and in the line of {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec d{/tex} , if they are collinear?
question_html:
Given {tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex} = 0, which of the following statements are correct:

  1. {tex}\vec a{/tex}, {tex}\vec b{/tex}, {tex}\vec c{/tex}, and {tex}\vec d{/tex}must each be a null vector.
  2. The magnitude of ({tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex}) equals the magnitude of ({tex}\vec b{/tex}+ {tex}\vec d{/tex}).
  3. The magnitude of '{tex}\vec a{/tex}' can never be greater than the sum of the magnitudes of {tex}\vec b{/tex}, {tex}\vec c{/tex}, and {tex}\vec d{/tex}.
  4. {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} must lie in the plane of {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec d{/tex} if  {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec d{/tex} are not collinear, and in the line of {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec d{/tex}, if they are collinear?
solution_text: Incorrect In order to make {tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex} = 0, it is not necessary to have all the four given vectors to be null vectors. There are many other combinations that can give the sum zero. Correct {tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex} = 0 {tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} = -( {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex} ) Taking modulus on both the sides, we get: | {tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} | = |-( {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex} )| = | {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex} | Hence, the magnitude of ( {tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} ) is the same as the magnitude of ( {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex} ). Correct {tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex} = 0 {tex}\vec a{/tex} = ( {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex} ) Taking modulus both sides, we get: | {tex}\vec a{/tex} | = | {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex} | | {tex}\vec a{/tex} | {tex}\leq{/tex} | {tex}\vec a{/tex} | + | {tex}\vec b{/tex} | + | {tex}\vec c{/tex} | ....(i) Equation (i) shows that the magnitude of a is equal to or less than the sum of the magnitudes of {tex}\vec b{/tex} , {tex}\vec c{/tex} , and {tex}\vec d{/tex} . Hence, the magnitude of a vector can never be greater than the sum of the magnitudes of b, c, and d. Correct For {tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex} = 0 {tex}\vec a{/tex} + ( {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} ) + {tex}\vec d{/tex} = 0 The resultant sum of the three vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} , ( {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} ), and d can be zero only if ( {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} ) lie in a plane containing a and d, assuming that these three vectors are represented by the three sides of a triangle. If a and d are collinear, then it implies that the vector ( {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} ) is in the line of a and d. This implication holds only then the vector sum of all the vectors will be zero.
solution_html:
1. Incorrect
    In order to make {tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex} = 0, it is not necessary to have all the four given vectors to be null vectors. There are many other combinations that can give the sum zero.
  2. Correct
    {tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex} = 0
    {tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} = -({tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex})
    Taking modulus on both the sides, we get:
    |{tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex}| = |-({tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex})| = |{tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex}|
    Hence, the magnitude of ({tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex}) is the same as the magnitude of ({tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex}).
  3. Correct
    {tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex} = 0
    {tex}\vec a{/tex} = ({tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex})
    Taking modulus both sides, we get:
    |{tex}\vec a{/tex}| = |{tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex}|
    |{tex}\vec a{/tex}| {tex}\leq{/tex} |{tex}\vec a{/tex}| + |{tex}\vec b{/tex}| + |{tex}\vec c{/tex}| ....(i)
    Equation (i) shows that the magnitude of a is equal to or less than the sum of the magnitudes of {tex}\vec b{/tex}, {tex}\vec c{/tex}, and {tex}\vec d{/tex}.
    Hence, the magnitude of a vector can never be greater than the sum of the magnitudes of b, c, and d.
  4. Correct
    For {tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex} + {tex}\vec d{/tex} = 0
    {tex}\vec a{/tex} + ({tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex}) + {tex}\vec d{/tex} = 0
    The resultant sum of the three vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex}, ({tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex}), and d can be zero only if ({tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex}) lie in a plane containing a and d, assuming that these three vectors are represented by the three sides of a triangle.
    If a and d are collinear, then it implies that the vector ({tex}\vec b{/tex} + {tex}\vec c{/tex}) is in the line of a and d. This implication holds only then the vector sum of all the vectors will be zero.

question_text: Three girls skating on a circular ice ground of radius 200 m start from a point P on the edge of the ground and reach a point Q diametrically opposite to P following different paths as shown in figure. What is the magnitude of the displacement vector for each? For which girl is this equal to the actual length of the path skated?
question_html: Three girls skating on a circular ice ground of radius 200 m start from a point P on the edge of the ground and reach a point Q diametrically opposite to P following different paths as shown in figure. What is the magnitude of the displacement vector for each? For which girl is this equal to the actual length of the path skated?
solution_text: Displacement is given by the minimum distance between the initial and final positions of a particle. In the given case, all the girls start from point P and reach point Q. The magnitudes of their displacements will be equal to the diameter of the ground. Radius of the ground = 200 m Diameter of the ground = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 200 = 400 m Hence, the magnitude of the displacement for each girl is 400 m. This is equal to the actual length of the path skated by girl B.
solution_html:
Displacement is given by the minimum distance between the initial and final positions of a particle. In the given case, all the girls start from point P and reach point Q. The magnitudes of their displacements will be equal to the diameter of the ground.
Radius of the ground = 200 m
Diameter of the ground = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 200 = 400 m
Hence, the magnitude of the displacement for each girl is 400 m. This is equal to the actual length of the path skated by girl B.

subject: Physics
question_text: A cyclist starts from the centre O of a circular park of radius 1 km, reaches the edge P of the park, then cycles along the circumference, and returns to the centre along QO as shown in figure. If the round trip takes 10 min, what is the net displacement, average velocity, and average speed of the cyclist?
question_html:
A cyclist starts from the centre O of a circular park of radius 1 km, reaches the edge P of the park, then cycles along the circumference, and returns to the centre along QO as shown in figure. If the round trip takes 10 min, what is the

  1. net displacement,
  2. average velocity, and
  3. average speed of the cyclist?
solution_text: Displacement is given by the minimum distance between the initial and final positions of a body. In the given case, the cyclist comes to the starting point after cycling for 10 minutes. Hence, his net displacement is zero. Average velocity is given by the relation: Average velocity {tex}= \frac { \text {net displacement} } { \text {total time interval} }{/tex} Since the net displacement of the cyclist is zero, his average velocity will also be zero. Average speed of the cyclist is given by the relation: Average speed {tex}= \frac { \text {total path length} } { \text {time interval} }{/tex} Total path length = OP + PQ + QO Total path length {tex}= 1 + \frac { 1 } { 4 } ( 2 \pi \times 1 ) + 1 = 2 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \pi = 3.570 \mathrm { km }{/tex} Time taken {tex}= 10 \mathrm { min } = \frac { 10 } { 60 } = \frac { 1 } { 6 } h{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Average speed {tex}= \frac { 3.570 } { \frac { 1 } { 6 } } = 21.42 \mathrm { km } / \mathrm { h }{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Displacement is given by the minimum distance between the initial and final positions of a body. In the given case, the cyclist comes to the starting point after cycling for 10 minutes.
    Hence, his net displacement is zero.
  2. Average velocity is given by the relation: Average velocity {tex}= \frac { \text {net displacement} } { \text {total time interval} }{/tex}
    Since the net displacement of the cyclist is zero, his average velocity will also be zero.
  3. Average speed of the cyclist is given by the relation: Average speed {tex}= \frac { \text {total path length} } { \text {time interval} }{/tex}
    Total path length = OP + PQ + QO
    Total path length {tex}= 1 + \frac { 1 } { 4 } ( 2 \pi \times 1 ) + 1 = 2 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \pi = 3.570 \mathrm { km }{/tex}
    Time taken {tex}= 10 \mathrm { min } = \frac { 10 } { 60 } = \frac { 1 } { 6 } h{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Average speed {tex}= \frac { 3.570 } { \frac { 1 } { 6 } } = 21.42 \mathrm { km } / \mathrm { h }{/tex}

Total path length = Circumference of the hexagon + PQ + QR Total path length= 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 500 + 500 + 500 = 4000 m The magnitude of displacement and the total path length corresponding to the required turns is shown in the given table Turn Magnitude of displacement (m) Total path length (m) Third 1000 1500 Sixth 0 3000 Eighth 866.03; 30 o 4000

The motorist take the sixth turn at point P, which is the starting point
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Magnitude of displacement = 0

question_text: A passenger arriving in a new town wishes to go from the station to a hotel located 10 km away on a straight road from the station. A dishonest cabman takes him along a circuitous path 23 km long and reaches the hotel in 28 min. What is the average speed of the taxi? the magnitude of average velocity? Are the two equal?
question_html:
A passenger arriving in a new town wishes to go from the station to a hotel located 10 km away on a straight road from the station. A dishonest cabman takes him along a circuitous path 23 km long and reaches the hotel in 28 min. What is

  1. the average speed of the taxi?
  2. the magnitude of average velocity? Are the two equal?
solution_text: Total distance travelled = 23 km Total times taken = 28 min {tex}= \frac { 28 } { 60 } h{/tex} Average speed of the taxi {tex}= \frac { \text { total dis tan }ce \text { travelled } } { \text {time interval} }{/tex} {tex}s _ { a v } = \frac { 23 } { \frac { 28 } { 60 } } = 49.29 \mathrm { km } / \mathrm { h }{/tex} Distance between the hotel and the station = 10 km = Displacement of the car Average velocity {tex}= \frac { \text {displacement} } { \text {time interval} } = \frac { 10 } { \frac { 28 } { 60 } } = 21.43 \mathrm { km } / h{/tex} Therefore, the two physical quantities (average speed and average velocity) are not equal.
solution_html:
1. Total distance travelled = 23 km
    Total times taken = 28 min {tex}= \frac { 28 } { 60 } h{/tex}
    Average speed of the taxi {tex}= \frac { \text { total dis tan }ce \text { travelled } } { \text {time interval} }{/tex}
    {tex}s _ { a v } = \frac { 23 } { \frac { 28 } { 60 } } = 49.29 \mathrm { km } / \mathrm { h }{/tex}
  2. Distance between the hotel and the station = 10 km = Displacement of the car
    Average velocity {tex}= \frac { \text {displacement} } { \text {time interval} } = \frac { 10 } { \frac { 28 } { 60 } } = 21.43 \mathrm { km } / h{/tex}
    Therefore, the two physical quantities (average speed and average velocity) are not equal.

question_text: The ceiling of a long hall is 25 m high. What is the maximum horizontal distance that a ball thrown with a speed of 40 ms -1 can go without hitting the ceiling of the hall?
question_html: The ceiling of a long hall is 25 m high. What is the maximum horizontal distance that a ball thrown with a speed of 40 ms-1 can go without hitting the ceiling of the hall?
solution_text: Speed of the ball, u = 40 m/s Maximum height, h = 25 m In Projectile motion, the maximum height reached by a body projected at an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} , is given by the relation: {tex}h = \frac { u ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } { 2 g }{/tex} {tex}25 = \frac { ( 40 ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \times 9.8 }{/tex} {tex}\sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 0.30625{/tex} {tex}\sin \theta = 0.5534{/tex} {tex}\therefore \theta = \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 0.5534 ) = 33.60 ^ { \circ }{/tex} Horizontal range, {tex}R = \frac { \mathrm { u } ^ { 2 } \sin 2 \theta } { \mathrm { g } }{/tex} {tex}R= \frac { ( 40 ) ^ { 2 } \times \sin( 2 \times 33.60) } { 9.8 }{/tex} {tex}R= \frac { 1600 \times \sin( 67.2) } { 9.8 }{/tex} {tex}R= \frac { 1600 \times 0.922 } { 9.8 } = 150.53 \mathrm { m }{/tex} so if ball is thrown at an angle smaller than {tex}33.60^\circ{/tex} ball will not hit the ceiling and maximum horizontal distance the ball will cover is {tex}\approx150.53\;m\;\;.{/tex}
solution_html:
Speed of the ball, u = 40 m/s

Maximum height, h = 25 m

In Projectile motion, the maximum height reached by a body projected at an angle {tex}\theta{/tex}, is given by the relation:
{tex}h = \frac { u ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } { 2 g }{/tex}
{tex}25 = \frac { ( 40 ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \times 9.8 }{/tex}
{tex}\sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 0.30625{/tex}
{tex}\sin \theta = 0.5534{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \theta = \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 0.5534 ) = 33.60 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
Horizontal range, {tex}R = \frac { \mathrm { u } ^ { 2 } \sin 2 \theta } { \mathrm { g } }{/tex}
{tex}R= \frac { ( 40 ) ^ { 2 } \times \sin( 2 \times 33.60) } { 9.8 }{/tex}
{tex}R= \frac { 1600 \times \sin( 67.2) } { 9.8 }{/tex}
{tex}R= \frac { 1600 \times 0.922 } { 9.8 } = 150.53 \mathrm { m }{/tex}    so if ball is thrown at an angle smaller than {tex}33.60^\circ{/tex} ball will not hit the ceiling and maximum horizontal distance the ball will cover is {tex}\approx150.53\;m\;\;.{/tex}

question_text: A cricketer can throw a ball to a maximum horizontal distance of 100 m. How much high above the ground can the cricketer throw the same ball ?
question_html: A cricketer can throw a ball to a maximum horizontal distance of 100 m. How much high above the ground can the cricketer throw the same ball ?
solution_text: Maximum horizontal distance, R = 100m The cricketer will only be able to throw the ball to the maximum horizontal distance when the angle of projection is 45 ∘ , i.e., = 45 ∘ . The max horizontal range for a projection velocity v is given by the relation: R max ​ = {tex}\frac{u^2}{g}{/tex} 100 = {tex}\frac{u^2}{g}{/tex} The ball will achieve the maximum height when it is thrown vertically upward. For such motion, the final velocity v is zero at the maximum height H. Acceleration, a = g Using the third equation of motion: v 2 − u 2 = −2gH H = {tex}\frac{u^2}{2g}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{100}{2}{/tex} = 50m
solution_html:
Maximum horizontal distance, R = 100m 
The cricketer will only be able to throw the ball to the maximum horizontal distance when the angle of projection is 45∘, i.e., = 45∘.
The max horizontal range for a projection velocity v is given by the relation:
Rmax​ = {tex}\frac{u^2}{g}{/tex}
100 = {tex}\frac{u^2}{g}{/tex}
The ball will achieve the maximum height when it is thrown vertically upward. For such motion, the final velocity v is zero at the maximum height H.
Acceleration, a = g
Using the third equation of motion:
v2 − u2 = −2gH
H = {tex}\frac{u^2}{2g}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{100}{2}{/tex} = 50m

question_text: A stone tied to the end of a string 80 cm long is whirled in a horizontal circle with a constant speed. If the stone makes 14 revolutions in 25 s, what is the magnitude and direction of acceleration of the stone?
question_html: A stone tied to the end of a string 80 cm long is whirled in a horizontal circle with a constant speed. If the stone makes 14 revolutions in 25 s, what is the magnitude and direction of acceleration of the stone?
solution_text: Given radius of the circular loop, r = length of the string = 80 cm = 0.8 m, frequency of revolutions, f = 14/25 rev / s -1 {tex}\therefore \omega = 2 \pi f = 2 \times \frac { 22 } { 7 } \times \frac { 14 } { 25 } = \frac { 88 } { 25 } \mathrm { rad } / \mathrm { s }{/tex} The centripetal acceleration {tex}a = \omega ^ { 2 } r = \left( \frac { 88 } { 25 } \right) ^ { 2 } \times 0.80 = 9.90 \mathrm { ms } ^ { - 2 }{/tex} The direction of centripetal acceleration is along the string, directed towards the centre of circular path.
solution_html:
Given radius of the circular loop, r = length of the string = 80 cm = 0.8 m, frequency of revolutions, f = 14/25 rev / s-1
{tex}\therefore \omega = 2 \pi f = 2 \times \frac { 22 } { 7 } \times \frac { 14 } { 25 } = \frac { 88 } { 25 } \mathrm { rad } / \mathrm { s }{/tex}
The centripetal acceleration
{tex}a = \omega ^ { 2 } r = \left( \frac { 88 } { 25 } \right) ^ { 2 } \times 0.80 = 9.90 \mathrm { ms } ^ { - 2 }{/tex}
The direction of centripetal acceleration is along the string, directed towards the centre of circular path.

question_text: An aircraft executes a horizontal loop of radius 1 km with a steady speed of 900 kmh -1 . Compare its centripetal acceleration with the acceleration due to gravity.
question_html: An aircraft executes a horizontal loop of radius 1 km with a steady speed of 900 kmh-1. Compare its centripetal acceleration with the acceleration due to gravity.
solution_text: Here, radius of the horizontal circular loop traversed by the aeroplane r = 1 km = 1000 m, with constant speed v = 900 km h -1 = {tex}\frac{900 \times ( 1000 \mathrm { m } )}{ ( 60 \times 60 \mathrm { s } )}{/tex} = 250 ms -1 We know that Centripetal acceleration, {tex}a = \frac { v ^ { 2 } } { r } = \frac { ( 250 ) ^ { 2 } } { 1000 }{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac { a } { g } = \frac { ( 250 ) ^ { 2 } } { 1000 } \times \frac { 1 } { 9.8 } = 6.38{/tex} Hence a c is 6.4 times more than the g in this case.
solution_html:
Here, radius of the horizontal circular loop traversed by the aeroplane r = 1 km = 1000 m,
with constant speed v = 900 km h-1 = {tex}\frac{900 \times ( 1000 \mathrm { m } )}{ ( 60 \times 60 \mathrm { s } )}{/tex}= 250 ms-1
We know that Centripetal acceleration, {tex}a = \frac { v ^ { 2 } } { r } = \frac { ( 250 ) ^ { 2 } } { 1000 }{/tex}
Now, {tex}\frac { a } { g } = \frac { ( 250 ) ^ { 2 } } { 1000 } \times \frac { 1 } { 9.8 } = 6.38{/tex}

Hence ac is 6.4 times more than the g in this case.

question_text: The net acceleration of a particle in a circular motion is always along the radius of the circle towards the centre.
question_html: The net acceleration of a particle in a circular motion is always along the radius of the circle towards the centre.

options.explanation: False; The net acceleration of a particle in circular motion is not always directed along the radius of the circle toward the center. It happens only in the case of uniform circular motion.

question_text: The velocity vector of a particle at a point is always along the tangent to the path of the particle at that point.
question_html: The velocity vector of a particle at a point is always along the tangent to the path of the particle at that point.

options.explanation: True; At a point on a circular path, a particle appears to move tangentially to the circular path. Hence, the velocity vector of the particle is always along the tangent at a point.

subject: Physics
question_text: The acceleration vector of a particle in uniform circular motion averaged over one cycle is a null vector.
question_html: The acceleration vector of a particle in uniform circular motion averaged over one cycle is a null vector.

options.explanation: True; In uniform circular motion (UCM), the direction of the acceleration vector points toward the centre of the circle. However, it constantly changes with time. The average of these vectors over one cycle is a null vector.

question_text: The position of a particle is given by r = 3.0t {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} - 2.0t 2 {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} + 4.0 {tex}\hat{k}{/tex} m where t is in seconds and the coefficients have the proper units for r to be in metres? Find the v and a of the particle? What is the magnitude and direction of velocity of the particle at t = 2.0 s?
question_html:
The position of a particle is given by r = 3.0t {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} - 2.0t2 {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} + 4.0 {tex}\hat{k}{/tex} m where t is in seconds and the coefficients have the proper units for r to be in metres?

  1. Find the v and a of the particle?
  2. What is the magnitude and direction of velocity of the particle at t = 2.0 s?
solution_text: {tex}\overline { v } ( t ) = ( 3.0 \hat { i } - 4.0 t \hat { j } ){/tex} The position of the particle is given by: {tex}\overline { r } = 3.0 t \hat { i } - 2.0 t ^ { 2 } \hat { j } + 4.0 \hat { k }{/tex} Velocity {tex}\overline{ v }{/tex} , of the particle is given as: {tex}\overline { v } = \frac { \overline { d r } } { d t } = \frac { d } { d t } \left( 3.0 t \hat { i } - 2.0 t ^ { 2 } \hat { j } - 4.0 k \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \overline { v } = 3.0 \hat { i } - 4.0 t \dot { j }{/tex} Acceleration {tex}\overline a{/tex} , of the particle is given as: {tex}\overline { \mathrm { a } } = \frac { \overline { \mathrm { dv } } } { \mathrm { dt } } = \frac { \mathrm { d } } { \mathrm { dt } } ( 3.0 \hat { \mathrm { i } } - 4.0 \mathrm { t } \hat { \mathrm { j } } ){/tex} {tex}\therefore \overline { \mathrm { a } } = - 4.0 \mathrm { j }{/tex} 8.54 m/s, 69.45° below the x-axis We have velocity sector, {tex}\overline { v } = 3.0 \hat { i } - 4.0t \hat{j}{/tex} At t = 2.0s : {tex}\overline { v } = 3.0 \hat { i } - 8.0 \hat { j }{/tex} The magnitude of velocity is given by: {tex}| \hat {v} | = \sqrt { 3 ^ { 2 } + ( - 8 ) ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { 73 } = 8.54 \mathrm { m } / \mathrm { s }{/tex} Direction, {tex}\theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \mathrm { v } _ { \mathrm { y } } } { \mathrm { v } _ { \mathrm { x } } } \right){/tex} {tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { - 8 } { 3 } \right) = - \tan ^ { - 1 } ( 2.667 ){/tex} = -69.45 o The negative sign indicates that the direction of velocity is below the x-axis.
solution_html:
1. {tex}\overline { v } ( t ) = ( 3.0 \hat { i } - 4.0 t \hat { j } ){/tex}
    The position of the particle is given by:
    {tex}\overline { r } = 3.0 t \hat { i } - 2.0 t ^ { 2 } \hat { j } + 4.0 \hat { k }{/tex}
    Velocity {tex}\overline{ v }{/tex}, of the particle is given as:
    {tex}\overline { v } = \frac { \overline { d r } } { d t } = \frac { d } { d t } \left( 3.0 t \hat { i } - 2.0 t ^ { 2 } \hat { j } - 4.0 k \right){/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \overline { v } = 3.0 \hat { i } - 4.0 t \dot { j }{/tex}
    Acceleration {tex}\overline a{/tex}, of the particle is given as:
    {tex}\overline { \mathrm { a } } = \frac { \overline { \mathrm { dv } } } { \mathrm { dt } } = \frac { \mathrm { d } } { \mathrm { dt } } ( 3.0 \hat { \mathrm { i } } - 4.0 \mathrm { t } \hat { \mathrm { j } } ){/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \overline { \mathrm { a } } = - 4.0 \mathrm { j }{/tex}
  2. 8.54 m/s, 69.45° below the x-axis
    We have velocity sector, {tex}\overline { v } = 3.0 \hat { i } - 4.0t \hat{j}{/tex}
    At
    t = 2.0s :
    {tex}\overline { v } = 3.0 \hat { i } - 8.0 \hat { j }{/tex}
    The magnitude of velocity is given by:
    {tex}| \hat {v} | = \sqrt { 3 ^ { 2 } + ( - 8 ) ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { 73 } = 8.54 \mathrm { m } / \mathrm { s }{/tex}
    Direction, {tex}\theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \mathrm { v } _ { \mathrm { y } } } { \mathrm { v } _ { \mathrm { x } } } \right){/tex}
    {tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { - 8 } { 3 } \right) = - \tan ^ { - 1 } ( 2.667 ){/tex}
    = -69.45o
    The negative sign indicates that the direction of velocity is below the x-axis.

question_text: A particle starts from the origin at t = 0 s with a velocity of {tex}10.0 \hat { \mathrm { j } } { m } / { s }{/tex} and moves in the x-y plane with a constant acceleration of {tex}( 8.0 \hat { i} + 2.0 \hat j ) m s ^ { - 2 }{/tex} . At what time is the x-coordinate of the particle 16 m? What is the y-coordinate of the particle at that time? What is the speed of the particle at the time?
question_html:
A particle starts from the origin at t = 0 s with a velocity of {tex}10.0 \hat { \mathrm { j } } { m } / { s }{/tex} and moves in the x-y plane with a constant acceleration of {tex}( 8.0 \hat { i} + 2.0 \hat j ) m s ^ { - 2 }{/tex}.

  1. At what time is the x-coordinate of the particle 16 m? What is the y-coordinate of the particle at that time?
  2. What is the speed of the particle at the time?
solution_text: Given: Velocity at time t = 0 is given as {tex} \vec { u } = 0 \hat { i } + 10 \hat { j } m / s{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} u x = 0m/s, u y = 10m/s Acceleration, {tex}\vec { a } = 8.0 \hat { i } + 2.0 \hat { j } m / s ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} a x = 8.0m/s 2 , a y = 2.0m/s 2 time taken by particle for x = 16 m Using equation, {tex}S = u t + \frac { 1 } { 2 } a t ^ { 2 }{/tex} along x axis {tex}x = u _ { x } t + \frac { 1 } { 2 } a _ { x } t ^ { 2 }{/tex} we get {tex}16 = ( 0 \times t ) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( 8 ) ( t ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} t = 2s y-coordinate at this time will be: {tex}y = u _ { y } t + \frac { 1 } { 2 } a _ { y } t ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}y = ( 10 \times 2 ) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( 2 ) ( 2 ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} y = 24 m Velocity along x and y-axis after time, t = 2s {tex}v _ { x } = u _ { x } + a _ { x } t \Rightarrow v _ { x } = 0 + ( 8 \times 2 ){/tex} v x = 16m/s {tex}v _ { y } = 10 + ( 2 \times 2 ){/tex} {tex}v _ { y } = 14 m / s{/tex} Net speed of the particle is: {tex}v = \sqrt { \left( v _ { x } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( v _ { y } \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}v = \sqrt { ( 16 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 14 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}v = 21.26 m / s{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: Velocity at time t = 0 is given as
  {tex} \vec { u } = 0 \hat { i } + 10 \hat { j } m / s{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} ux = 0m/s, uy = 10m/s
Acceleration, {tex}\vec { a } = 8.0 \hat { i } + 2.0 \hat { j } m / s ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} ax = 8.0m/s2, ay = 2.0m/s2

  1. time taken by particle for x = 16 m
    Using equation, {tex}S = u t + \frac { 1 } { 2 } a t ^ { 2 }{/tex} along x axis
    {tex}x = u _ { x } t + \frac { 1 } { 2 } a _ { x } t ^ { 2 }{/tex} we get 
    {tex}16 = ( 0 \times t ) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( 8 ) ( t ) ^ { 2 }{/tex}
    t = 2s
    y-coordinate at this time will be:
    {tex}y = u _ { y } t + \frac { 1 } { 2 } a _ { y } t ^ { 2 }{/tex}
    {tex}y = ( 10 \times 2 ) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( 2 ) ( 2 ) ^ { 2 }{/tex}
    y = 24 m
  2. Velocity along x and y-axis after time, t = 2s
    {tex}v _ { x } = u _ { x } + a _ { x } t \Rightarrow v _ { x } = 0 + ( 8 \times 2 ){/tex}
    vx = 16m/s
    {tex}v _ { y } = 10 + ( 2 \times 2 ){/tex}
    {tex}v _ { y } = 14 m / s{/tex}
    Net speed of the particle is:
    {tex}v = \sqrt { \left( v _ { x } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( v _ { y } \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
    {tex}v = \sqrt { ( 16 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 14 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
    {tex}v = 21.26 m / s{/tex}

question_text: {tex}\hat { i }{/tex} and {tex}\hat j{/tex} are unit vectors along x and y-axes respectively. What is the magnitude and direction of vectors {tex}\hat i{/tex} + {tex}\hat j {/tex} and {tex}\hat { i }{/tex} - {tex}\hat j{/tex} ? What are the components of a vector A = {tex}2 \hat { \mathbf { i } } + 3 \hat { \mathbf { j } }{/tex} along the direction {tex}\hat i{/tex} + {tex}\hat j {/tex} and {tex}\hat { i }{/tex} - {tex}\hat j{/tex} ?
question_html: {tex}\hat { i }{/tex} and {tex}\hat j{/tex} are unit vectors along x and y-axes respectively. What is the magnitude and direction of vectors {tex}\hat i{/tex}  + {tex}\hat j {/tex} and {tex}\hat { i }{/tex} - {tex}\hat j{/tex}? What are the components of a vector A = {tex}2 \hat { \mathbf { i } } + 3 \hat { \mathbf { j } }{/tex} along the direction {tex}\hat i{/tex}  + {tex}\hat j {/tex} and {tex}\hat { i }{/tex} - {tex}\hat j{/tex}?
solution_text: As we know {tex}\hat { i }{/tex} and {tex}\hat j{/tex} are unit vectors, Magnitude of {tex}( \hat { i } + \hat { j } ) = \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} units If vector {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } + \hat { \mathbf { j } } ){/tex} makes an angle of {tex}\theta{/tex} with the x - axis, then {tex}\tan \theta = \frac { A _ { y } } { A _ { x } } = \frac { 1 } { 1 }{/tex} = 1 = tan 45° or {tex}\theta{/tex} = 45° Similarly, magnitude of {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } - \hat { \mathbf { j } } ) = \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} If vector {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } - \hat { \mathbf { j } } ){/tex} makes an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} , with x - axis, then {tex}\tan \theta = \frac { A _ { y } } { A _ { x } } = \frac { ( - 1 ) } { 1 } = - 1{/tex} {tex}= - \tan 45 ^ { \circ } \Rightarrow \theta = - 45 ^ { \circ } {/tex} with {tex}\hat i{/tex} Hence, resultant vector {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } - \hat { \mathbf { j } } ){/tex} makes an angle of 45° from x-axis in negative direction. To determine the component of A = {tex}2 \hat { \mathbf { i } } + 3 \hat { \mathbf { j } }{/tex} in the direction of {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } + \hat { \mathbf { j } } ){/tex} Let us assume B = {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } + \hat { \mathbf { j } } ){/tex} , then A.B = AB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = (A cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ). B or Acos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}​​\frac { \mathbf { A } \cdot \mathbf { B } } { B }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A \cos \theta = \frac { \mathbf { A } \cdot \mathbf { B } } { B } = \frac { ( 2 \hat { \mathbf { i } } + 3 \hat { \mathbf { j } } ) \cdot ( \hat { \mathbf { i } } + \hat { \mathbf { j } } ) } { \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 \hat { \mathbf { i } } \cdot \hat { \mathbf { i } } + 3 \hat { \mathbf { j } } \cdot \hat { \mathbf { j } } } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 + 3 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { 5 } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} . This is the component of vector A in the direction of {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } + \hat { \mathbf { j } } ){/tex} Unit vector along {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } + \hat { \mathbf { j } } ) , \hat { \mathbf { n } } = \frac { ( \hat { \mathbf { i } } + \hat { \mathbf { j } } ) } { | \hat { \mathbf { i } } + \hat { \mathbf { j } } | } = \frac { ( \hat { \mathbf { i } } + \hat { \mathbf { j } } ) } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} Component of A along {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } - \hat { \mathbf { j } } ){/tex} The magnitude of the component of A in the direction of {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } - \hat { \mathbf { j } } ) = \frac { ( 2 \hat { \mathbf { i } } + 3 \hat { \mathbf { j } } ) \cdot ( \hat { \mathbf { i } } - \hat { \mathbf { j } } ) } { | \hat { \mathbf { i } } - \hat { \mathbf { j } } | } = \frac { 2 \hat { \mathbf { i } } \cdot \hat { \mathbf { i } } - 3 \hat { \mathbf { j } } \hat { \mathbf { j } } } { \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 - 3 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { -1 } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} . This is the component of vector A in the direction of {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } - \hat { \mathbf { j } } ){/tex} .
solution_html:
1. As we know {tex}\hat { i }{/tex} and {tex}\hat j{/tex} are unit vectors, Magnitude of {tex}( \hat { i } + \hat { j } ) = \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} units
    If vector {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } + \hat { \mathbf { j } } ){/tex} makes an angle of {tex}\theta{/tex} with the x - axis, then
    {tex}\tan \theta = \frac { A _ { y } } { A _ { x } } = \frac { 1 } { 1 }{/tex} = 1 = tan 45° or {tex}\theta{/tex} = 45°
  2. Similarly, magnitude of
    {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } - \hat { \mathbf { j } } ) = \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { 2 }{/tex}
    If vector {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } - \hat { \mathbf { j } } ){/tex} makes an angle {tex}\theta{/tex}, with x - axis, then
    {tex}\tan \theta = \frac { A _ { y } } { A _ { x } } = \frac { ( - 1 ) } { 1 } = - 1{/tex}
    {tex}= - \tan 45 ^ { \circ } \Rightarrow \theta = - 45 ^ { \circ } {/tex} with {tex}\hat i{/tex}
    Hence, resultant vector {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } - \hat { \mathbf { j } } ){/tex} makes an angle of 45° from x-axis in negative direction.
  3. To determine the component of A = {tex}2 \hat { \mathbf { i } } + 3 \hat { \mathbf { j } }{/tex} in the direction of {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } + \hat { \mathbf { j } } ){/tex}
    Let us assume B = {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } + \hat { \mathbf { j } } ){/tex}, then 
    A.B = AB cos{tex}\theta{/tex} = (A cos{tex}\theta{/tex}). B
    or Acos{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}​​\frac { \mathbf { A } \cdot \mathbf { B } } { B }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow A \cos \theta = \frac { \mathbf { A } \cdot \mathbf { B } } { B } = \frac { ( 2 \hat { \mathbf { i } } + 3 \hat { \mathbf { j } } ) \cdot ( \hat { \mathbf { i } } + \hat { \mathbf { j } } ) } { \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { 2 \hat { \mathbf { i } } \cdot \hat { \mathbf { i } } + 3 \hat { \mathbf { j } } \cdot \hat { \mathbf { j } } } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { 2 + 3 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { 5 } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}. This is the component of vector A in the direction of {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } + \hat { \mathbf { j } } ){/tex}
  4. Unit vector along {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } + \hat { \mathbf { j } } ) , \hat { \mathbf { n } } = \frac { ( \hat { \mathbf { i } } + \hat { \mathbf { j } } ) } { | \hat { \mathbf { i } } + \hat { \mathbf { j } } | } = \frac { ( \hat { \mathbf { i } } + \hat { \mathbf { j } } ) } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}
    Component of A along {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } - \hat { \mathbf { j } } ){/tex}
    The magnitude of the component of A in the direction of
    {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } - \hat { \mathbf { j } } ) = \frac { ( 2 \hat { \mathbf { i } } + 3 \hat { \mathbf { j } } ) \cdot ( \hat { \mathbf { i } } - \hat { \mathbf { j } } ) } { | \hat { \mathbf { i } } - \hat { \mathbf { j } } | } = \frac { 2 \hat { \mathbf { i } } \cdot \hat { \mathbf { i } } - 3 \hat { \mathbf { j } } \hat { \mathbf { j } } } { \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 - 3 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { -1 } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}. This is the component of vector A in the direction of {tex}( \hat { \mathbf { i } } - \hat { \mathbf { j } } ){/tex}.

question_text: For any arbitrary motion in space, {tex}\mathbf{v}_{\text {average }}=(1 / 2)\left(\mathbf{v}\left(t_{1}\right)+\mathbf{v}\left(t_{2}\right)\right){/tex}
question_html: For any arbitrary motion in space, {tex}\mathbf{v}_{\text {average }}=(1 / 2)\left(\mathbf{v}\left(t_{1}\right)+\mathbf{v}\left(t_{2}\right)\right){/tex}

options.explanation: False, If the velocity of the particle is v (t 1 ) at time t = t 1 and velocity is v (t 2 ) at time interval t = t 2 then the average velocity of the particle is denoted by v average {tex} = \left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)\left( {v({t_1} + v({t_2})} \right){/tex} in case of arbitrary motion when acceleration may or may not be constant the above relation is incorrect.

question_text: For any arbitrary motion in space, {tex}{{\mathbf{v}}_{{\text{avernge }}}} = \frac{{\left[ {{\mathbf{r}}\left( {{t_2}} \right) - {\mathbf{r}}\left( {{t_1}} \right)} \right]}}{{\left( {{t_2} - {t_1}} \right)}}{/tex}
question_html: For any arbitrary motion in space,{tex}{{\mathbf{v}}_{{\text{avernge }}}} = \frac{{\left[ {{\mathbf{r}}\left( {{t_2}} \right) - {\mathbf{r}}\left( {{t_1}} \right)} \right]}}{{\left( {{t_2} - {t_1}} \right)}}{/tex}

options.explanation: True, We know the average velocity for any type of motion is given by v average = total displacement / total time So, {tex}\left[ {\frac{{r({t_2}) - r({t_1})}}{{({t_2} - {t_1})}}} \right]{/tex} denotes the average velocity of the particle v average where, r(t 1 ) and r(t 2 ) are the position vector of the particle at time interval t = t 1 and t = t 2 respectively. So r(t 2 ) - r(t 1 ) is the change in position of a particle or the displacement of the particle and ( t 2 – t 1 ) is the time taken for that displacement

question_text: For any arbitrary motion in space, v(t) = v(0) + at
question_html: For any arbitrary motion in space, v(t) = v(0) + at

options.explanation: False, Here v(0) denotes the velocity of the particle at a time interval, t = 0 i.e. it denotes the initial velocity of the particle v(t) is the velocity of the particle at any time interval t seconds and a is the acceleration, so it is the first equation of motion v = u + at the above equation is valid only in case of uniform motion i.e. when the acceleration of the particle is constant but in case of arbitrary motion it may or may not be constant so above equation is not always valid.

question_html: For any arbitrary motion in space, {tex}r(t) = r(0) + v(0)t + \left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)a\;{t^2}{/tex}

options.explanation: False, Here r (0) denotes the position of the particle at time interval t = 0, r (t) is the position of the particle at time interval t, a is the acceleration of the particle r (t) = r (0) + v (0) t + (1/2) at 2 can be rearranged as r (t) - r (0) = v (0) t + (1/2) at 2 so r (t) - r (0) is the change in position of particle or displacement v (0) is the velocity of particle at time interval t = 0 or initial velocity of the particle i.e. this equation represents the equation of motion {tex}S = ut + \left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)a{t^2}{/tex} but the above equation is valid only in case of uniform motion i.e. when the acceleration of the particle is constant but in case of arbitrary motion it may or may not be constant so above equation is not always valid.

question_text: An aircraft is flying at a height of 3400 m above the ground. If the angle subtended at a ground observation point by the aircraft positions 10.0 s apart is 30 o , what is the speed of the aircraft?
question_html: An aircraft is flying at a height of 3400 m above the ground. If the angle subtended at a ground observation point by the aircraft positions 10.0 s apart is 30o, what is the speed of the aircraft?
solution_text: The positions of the observer and the aircraft are shown in the given figure. The aircraft is following path PRQ and observer is at 'O'. Height of the aircraft from ground, OR = 3400 m Angle subtended between the position, {tex}\angle P O Q = 30 ^ { \circ }{/tex} Time interval, t = 10 s In {tex}\Delta P R O{/tex} {tex}\tan 15 ^ { \circ } = \frac { P R } { O R }{/tex} PR = OR tan 15 o {tex}PR= 3400 \times \tan 15 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}\Delta P R O{/tex} is similar to {tex}\Delta R Q O{/tex} {tex}\therefore P R = R Q{/tex} P Q = PR + RQ {tex}P Q = 2 P R = 2 \times 3400 \tan 15 ^ { \circ }{/tex} ​​​​​​​ {tex}P Q = 6800 \times 0.268 = 1822.4 \mathrm { m }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Speed of the aircraft {tex}\begin{array}{l}=\frac{distance}{time\;interval}=\frac{1822.4}{10}=182.24\;\mathrm m/\mathrm s\\\end{array}{/tex} ​​​​​​​
solution_html:
The positions of the observer and the aircraft are shown in the given figure. The aircraft is following path PRQ and observer is at 'O'.

Height of the aircraft from ground, OR = 3400 m
Angle subtended between the position, {tex}\angle P O Q = 30 ^ { \circ }{/tex} 
Time interval, t = 10 s
In {tex}\Delta P R O{/tex}
{tex}\tan 15 ^ { \circ } = \frac { P R } { O R }{/tex}
PR = OR tan 15o
{tex}PR= 3400 \times \tan 15 ^ { \circ }{/tex} 
{tex}\Delta P R O{/tex} is similar to {tex}\Delta R Q O{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore P R = R Q{/tex} 
P Q = PR + RQ
{tex}P Q = 2 P R = 2 \times 3400 \tan 15 ^ { \circ }{/tex} ​​​​​​​
{tex}P Q = 6800 \times 0.268 = 1822.4 \mathrm { m }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Speed of the aircraft {tex}\begin{array}{l}=\frac{distance}{time\;interval}=\frac{1822.4}{10}=182.24\;\mathrm m/\mathrm s\\\end{array}{/tex}​​​​​​​
